claim: The harness (system prompt, tools, workflows, rules) matters as much or more than the model choice for reliable AI coding
actionable: Invest in harness quality (system prompts, tool definitions, guard rails) before spending on expensive models; a good harness + cheap model beats bad harness + expensive model
source_title: Omnigent: The New Meta-Harness for EVERY Coding Agent

claim: Meta-harnesses orchestrate multiple AI coding assistants (Claude Code, Codex, Pi) with different strengths for different workflow phases, enabling model diversity
actionable: Use meta-harnesses to delegate implementation to one model and code review to another; this separation reduces LLM bias and improves quality
source_title: Omnigent: The New Meta-Harness for EVERY Coding Agent

claim: Code review in a separate agent session from implementation prevents LLM bias buildup that occurs when same model reviews its own code
actionable: Never have the same LLM/agent session handle both code implementation and review; use separate sessions or models for review to catch more bugs
source_title: Omnigent: The New Meta-Harness for EVERY Coding Agent

claim: Orchestrator agents can have guard rail policies (e.g., deny force git pushes) that apply across any underlying agent without reconfiguring each one
actionable: Define high-level policies in your meta-harness/orchestrator layer; these apply to all delegated agents without duplicating config in each tool
source_title: Omnigent: The New Meta-Harness for EVERY Coding Agent

claim: Multi-agent debate workflows (two models arguing opposing viewpoints before synthesizing) produce higher-quality conclusions than single-model analysis
actionable: Use meta-harnesses to set up debate workflows: have two models argue different solutions, then synthesize the best parts with an orchestrator agent
source_title: Omnigent: The New Meta-Harness for EVERY Coding Agent
